title-group.article-title: Development and preliminary validation of a PICC-related venous thrombosis risk prediction model for cancer patients: A systematic review and meta-analysis predominantly based on Chinese populations

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Nursing, National Cancer Center/National Clinical Research Center for Cancer/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China

bDepartment of Hepatobiliary Surgery, National Cancer Center/National Clinical Research Center for Cancer/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China

cDepartment of Thoracic Surgery, National Cancer Center/National Clinical Research Center for Cancer/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China

dDepartment of Radiation Oncology, National Cancer Center/National Clinical Research Center for Cancer/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China

eDepartment of Medical Oncology, National Cancer Center/National Clinical Research Center for Cancer/Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College, Beijing, China

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To identify key risk factors for peripherally inserted central catheter -related deep venous thrombosis (PICC-RVT) in patients with cancer through a systematic review and meta-analysis, develop risk prediction model and validate its performance.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic literature search was performed in Pubmed, EMBASE, Web of Science (core collection), Scopus, CINAHL and Ovid databases from the time of their inception to June 2025. Study quality was assessed using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) checklist. Meta-analysis was performed using RevMan 5.4 statistical software to identify independent risk factors for PICC-RVT in adult patients with cancer, then the effect of each independent risk factor was determined using β - formation conversion for developing the risk predictive model. Finally, we collected the clinical datal of 338 adult patients with cancer who underwent PICC catheterization from June 2024 to May 2025 to evaluate the predictive performance of the risk predictive model by drawing the receiver of curve (ROC).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 32 cohort studies involving 28,813 individuals (28 from China, 2 from UK, 1 each from US and Canada) were included. Twenty-one independent risk factors were identified through meta-analysis. The risk prediction model was developed using β - coefficient transformation: pooled odds ratios (ORs) from meta-analysis were converted to β coefficients through natural logarithmic transformation (β = ln[OR]), then each β - coefficient was multiplied by 10 and rounded to one decimal place to create a point-based scoring system. The total risk score was calculated by summing individual factor scores. Preliminary external validation was conducted in 338 patients with cancer (15 thrombosis events, 4.4% incidence) from a single Chinese center, yielding an area under curve (AUC) of 0.792 (95% [confidence interval (CI)] 0.653–0.931). The model showed acceptable calibration (Hosmer–Lemeshow P = 0.082) but validation was underpowered (15 events vs. recommended 100+ events for precise performance estimation).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This study developed a PICC-RVT risk prediction model based primarily on Chinese patients with cancer. The model demonstrated moderate discrimination in preliminary single-center validation, but requires multi-center validation with adequate event numbers before clinical implementation. The model provides a framework for PICC-RVT risk stratification in Chinese and similar healthcare settings.

sec.title: Systematic review registration
sec.p: PROSPERO (CRD420250651190).

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: In recent years, owing to the numerous advantages of peripherally inserted central catheters (PICCs), including ease of insertion, high safety, cost-effectiveness, and their suitability for long-term and repeated use, they have increasingly more popular than other central venous catheters (CVCs) in medical practice choices.1, 2, 3 In addition, the establishment of nurse-led PICC teams has facilitated the broader and more convenient utilization of PICC across diverse clinical settings.4 Despite their advantages, the widespread use of PICCs continues to expose patients to a range of high-risk complications, thereby increasing mortality rates, morbidity, length of hospital stay, and medical costs, etc.5,6

PICC-related deep venous thrombosis (PICC-RVT) is one of the most severe complications associated with PICC placement, characterized by the formation of a thrombus within deep veins such as the subclavian vein or internal jugular vein following PICC insertion.7 The diagnosis of PICC-RVT is typically confirmed using imaging techniques, including ultrasound, venography, or computed tomography (CT).8 With the increasing use of PICC in tumor chemotherapy, parenteral nutrition, and long-term infusion therapy, there has been a notable increase in the incidence of PICC-RVT among patients with cancer.9 Studies have shown that the incidence of PICC-RVT in patients with cancer ranges from 8.6% to 15.3%, which is significantly higher than that in non-cancer populations (3.2–5.8%), and becoming the leading complication that leads to unplanned catheter removal, treatment interruption and increased medical costs.10 Furthermore, while symptomatic PICC-RVT occurs at an incidence rate of 6–25% and is typically associated with clinical manifestations such as unilateral upper limb swelling, discomfort, and erythema.11,12 However, early-stage PICC-RVT often presents without noticeable clinical symptoms and has an incidence rate ranging from 35% to 71.9%.13 Given the complexity of this issue, identifying the risk factors contributing to PICC-RVT formation in patients with cancer and developing a robust risk prediction model are critical priorities requiring immediate attention.

Given the predominance of PICC use and related research in Asia–Pacific regions, particularly China, developing an evidence-based risk prediction model specifically derived from and validated in these populations is of paramount importance. Chinese patients with cancer may exhibit distinct risk profiles due to differences in cancer epidemiology (higher prevalence of gastric and liver cancers), healthcare delivery systems (such as differences in routine ultrasound screening protocols for catheter-related complications, variations in nursing-to-patient ratios for catheter maintenance, and the extent of family caregiver involvement in home catheter care), baseline thrombotic risk (hepatitis B prevalence affecting coagulation pathways), and chemotherapy regimens commonly used in the region. While existing models like Caprini were developed primarily in Western populations, their performance and calibration in Asia–Pacific settings remain uncertain. More critically, these models were not developed through systematic synthesis of risk factors identified across multiple studies in Chinese populations. Therefore, conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis to pool effect estimates from Chinese studies provides the essential evidence base for developing a prediction model that reflects the actual risk factor profiles and effect magnitudes observed in this population. This approach ensures that the model is built on comprehensive, regionally-representative evidence rather than extrapolated from Western data.

Numerous studies have identified predictive factors associated with PICC-RVT and established corresponding risk prediction models, such as those developed by Seeley14 and Michigan,15 Peng et al.,16 and Hu et al.17 Nevertheless, several limitations hinder the applicability and reliability of these models. For example, some were derived from retrospective studies, which may be susceptible to selection bias.14, 15, 16 Moreover, certain models lack comprehensive inclusion of potentially relevant risk factors that could serve as important predictors.14,17 In addition, the clinical implementation of these models remains limited, and their predictive accuracy and real-world utility warrant further empirical validation. Notably, current clinical guidelines do not advocate for the use of any specific prediction model for PICC-RVT.18 While the Caprini model has been occasionally proposed as a potential option, its application may result in excessive preventive measures, thereby posing practical challenges.19,20

Virchow's triad is a foundational theory in the study of thrombosis. This theory identifies three primary factors contributing to thrombosis: vascular endothelial injury (e.g., venipuncture or chemotherapeutic usage), stasis of venous circulation (e.g., lack of mobility or obesity), and hypercoagulability (e.g., high D-dimer levels or heparin-induced thrombocytopenia).21 In patients with malignant tumors, both the systemic mechanisms of cancer-related thrombosis and local vascular injury factors associated with catheterization may contribute to the development of PICC-related venous thrombosis.22 However, the available evidence on risk factors for catheter-related thrombosis remains fragmented across individual studies with varying sample sizes, study designs, and inconsistent effect estimates. The clinical relevance and pooled effect size of individual risk factors have not been systematically quantified through meta-analysis. This evidence gap necessitates a comprehensive systematic review and meta-analysis to: (1) provide precise pooled estimates of effect sizes for each risk factor, (2) identify factors with consistent associations across multiple studies, and (3) establish an evidence-based foundation for developing a clinically applicable prediction model. Previous studies have investigated multiple risk factors for PRT; however, the findings have been inconsistent and therefore remain inconclusive.23,24

Therefore, this study aimed to: (1) systematically identify and quantify risk factors for PICC-RVT in patients with cancer through comprehensive meta-analysis of existing cohort studies; (2) develop an evidence-based risk prediction model using pooled effect estimates from meta-analysis, with risk factors organized according to Virchow's triad and cancer-specific mechanisms; and (3) conduct preliminary validation in a Chinese cancer center to assess model performance. Given the concentration of available evidence from Chinese healthcare settings, we specifically examine model performance in this population while acknowledging that external validation in diverse Asia–Pacific settings will be necessary to establish broader transportability.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This prediction model development and validation study was conducted following the Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis (TRIPOD) statement.25 The systematic review and meta-analysis component, which served as the evidence base for model development, followed the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses 2020 (PRISMA 2020) guidelines26 and has been registered on PROSPERO (CRD420250651190).

sec.title: Theoretical framework
sec.p: Risk factor selection and model development were guided by Virchow's triad, the foundational theory of thrombosis formation encompassing three pathophysiological mechanisms: (1) venous stasis (e.g., reduced limb mobility, obesity), (2) vascular endothelial injury (e.g., catheter insertion, chemotherapy), and (3) hypercoagulability (e.g., elevated D-dimer, cancer-related coagulation activation). We systematically included risk factors representing each component of this triad, as well as cancer-specific factors (tumor type, stage, metastases) and treatment-related factors (surgery, chemotherapy regimens) that may amplify thrombotic risk through multiple mechanisms. This theoretical framework ensures biological plausibility and clinical relevance of included predictors rather than purely empirical selection.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: We comprehensively searched the electronic databases including Pubmed, EMBASE, Web of Science (core collection), Scopus, CINAHL and Ovid databases from the time of their inception to June 2025. The search was limited to English language publications. A comprehensive search strategy was employed, combining free text words and MeSH headings with the terms: “peripherally inserted central catheter line insertion”, “PICC”, “thrombosis”, “upper extremity deep vein thrombosis”, “catheter-related thrombosis”, “PICC-related VTE”, “neoplasms”, “tumor”, “risk” and “risk factors” (Supplementary Table S1). Additionally, we reviewed the reference lists of retrieved research articles and review papers to ensure that no relevant studies were overlooked. While our search strategy included ‘risk’ and ‘risk factors’ terms, we employed several approaches to ensure comprehensive retrieval: (1) we searched for all studies involving ‘PICC’ combined with ‘thrombosis’ which captured observational studies reporting risk factors even without explicit ‘risk’ terminology; (2) we imposed no restrictions on study design terms; (3) we manually screened reference lists of all retrieved articles; and (4) we searched six databases to maximize coverage. Our search yielded 2141 initial records ultimately including 32 studies, suggesting adequate sensitivity.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: After deduplication using EndNote software, two independent reviewers assessed the eligibility of publications according to predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria by screening titles and abstracts. Any disagreements were resolved through discussion, with a third reviewer consulted if consensus could not be reached. Full texts of eligible studies were retrieved and evaluated to determine the final selection for analysis. In cases where information was insufficient, corresponding authors were contacted via email to request supplementary data.

The inclusion criteria for studies were: (1) studies involving patients with malignant neoplasm; (2) the outcome of interest was PICC-RVT, with diagnostic criteria including visible venous lumen dilation, thrombus echoes within the venous lumen, absence of vessel deformation or compression under probe pressure, and filling defects, detours, or lack of blood flow signals in the vessel. PICC-RVT was diagnosed using objective imaging methods including Doppler ultrasound, venography, or computed tomography, with diagnostic criteria including visible venous lumen dilation, thrombus echoes within the venous lumen, absence of vessel deformation under probe pressure, and filling defects or absence of blood flow signals; (3) an observational study design; (4) reported risk factors for PICC-RVT and provided odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for PICC-RVT risk factors, or where ORs and CIs could be calculated from reported data. The exclusion criteria were: (1) duplicate publications; (2) the full text could not be obtained, which prevented the extraction of the OR values and 95% confidence intervals (CIs), despite attempts to contact the authors via email; (3) not written in English and of low quality.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction was performed independently by two authors using a standardized data extraction form based on the CHARMS checklist.27 The extracted information encompassed the first author's name, publication year, country, sample size, study methodology, duration of catheterization, and specific findings associated with PICC-RVT. Furthermore, key statistical indicators related to PICC-RVT risk factors were derived from multivariate logistic regression analyses. These indicators comprised P-values, 95% confidence intervals (CIs), as well as adjusted measures such as adjusted odds ratios (aORs), adjusted risk ratios (aRRs), or adjusted hazard ratios (aHRs). For each risk factor reported, we extracted only adjusted odds ratios (aORs), adjusted risk ratios (aRRs), or adjusted hazard ratios (aHRs) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs) and p-values from multivariable logistic or Cox regression analyses to control for confounding. When multiple models were reported, we extracted estimates from the most fully adjusted model. Unadjusted estimates were not included in meta-analysis.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: Two researchers independently assessed the quality and risk of bias of the cohort studies using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS).28 This scale is a detailed 9-point tool designed for evaluating non-randomized studies, assessing study quality across seven dimensions, including selection of the study population, comparability between groups, and exposure factors. Studies are categorized into three levels of bias risk: low (≤ 4 points), moderate (5–6 points), or high (≥ 7 points). Following the initial evaluation, the researchers cross-verified their results, and any inconsistencies were resolved through consultation with a third researcher.

We used the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE)29 to conduct the quality assessment of evidence. The GRADE evaluates whether to downgrade the quality of evidence based on five key domains: risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, imprecision, and other considerations (i.e., publication bias). According to the GRADE rating system, the overall quality of evidence is classified as “very low”, “low”,“moderate” or “high”.

sec.title: Synthesis methods
sec.p: Statistical analysis was performed using Revman5.4 software. A systematic evaluation of the clinical and methodological characteristics of the studies was conducted, followed by an assessment of statistical heterogeneity using the χ2 test and I2 statistic. If P > 0.1 and I2 ≤ 50%, this suggests that the heterogeneity among studies is within an acceptable range, and a fixed-effect model should be employed for the meta-analysis. Conversely, if P ≤ 0.1 and I2 > 50%, this indicates that there is substantial heterogeneity among studies, necessitating the use of a random-effects model for the meta-analysis. If subgroup analyses failed to elucidate the source of heterogeneity, sensitivity analyses were performed to assess the robustness of the results. Risk factors reported in only one study were excluded from meta-analysis and described qualitatively. Risk factors reported in two or more studies were included in meta-analysis, as two studies represent the minimum for statistical pooling and heterogeneity assessment. For meta-analyses with ≥ 3 studies, we additionally calculated 95% prediction intervals to estimate the expected range of effects in future settings, using the method described by IntHout et al.30 We acknowledge that effect estimates from 2 - study meta-analyses have wider confidence intervals and limited precision, which was considered in model factor selection.

sec.title: Subgroup and sensitivity analyses
sec.p: For risk factors with substantial heterogeneity (I2 > 50%), we conducted pre-specified subgroup analyses based on:
Cancer type: Hematological malignancies vs. solid tumors (for D-dimer and other coagulation markers, given distinct coagulation profiles).
Surgery type: Breast surgery vs. other surgeries (due to different surgical trauma levels and immobility patterns).
Study design: Prospective vs. retrospective cohorts (to assess design-related bias).
When subgroup analyses did not fully explain heterogeneity, we performed sensitivity analyses by:
Sequentially removing individual studies to assess influence of outliers.
Restricting analyses to high-quality studies (NOS ≥ 7).
Restricting to studies with similar PICC-RVT diagnostic criteria (e.g., routine screening vs. symptomatic testing).

Geographic distribution subgroup analysis: Given the geographic concentration of included studies (28 from China, 4 from other countries), we conducted exploratory subgroup meta-analysis comparing risk factor effect sizes between Chinese and non-Chinese studies. For risk factors reported in both geographic regions, we performed subgroup meta-analysis using the Chi-square test for interaction (P < 0.10 indicating significant heterogeneity between geographic subgroups). This analysis aimed to assess whether risk factor effects varied systematically by geographic region, informing the model's potential transportability beyond Chinese healthcare settings. This analysis addresses generalizability/transportability rather than robustness of individual estimates, warranting separate presentation from other sensitivity analyses.
Results of all subgroup and sensitivity analyses are presented in Supplementary Table S2.

sec.title: Geographic distribution analysis
sec.p: Given the geographic concentration of included studies, we conducted sensitivity analyses comparing risk factor effect sizes between Chinese studies (n = 28) and non-Chinese studies (n = 4). For risk factors reported in both regions, we performed subgroup meta-analysis using the Chi-square test for interaction (P < 0.10 indicating significant heterogeneity between geographic subgroups). This analysis aimed to assess whether risk factor effects varied systematically by geographic region, informing the model's potential transportability beyond Chinese healthcare settings.

sec.title: Derivation of β coefficients
sec.p:
The β coefficients for the prediction model were derived exclusively from pooled odds ratios (ORs) obtained through meta-analysis. The process was as follows:1.
For factors with meta-analysis (≥ 2 studies): Pooled ORs with 95% CIs from fixed-effect or random-effects meta-analysis were converted to β coefficients through natural logarithmic transformation: β = ln(OR). For example, if meta-analysis yielded OR = 2.18 for previous VTE event, then β = ln(2.18) = 0.779.2.
For factors reported in only one study: These factors were excluded from the prediction model because: (a) single-study estimates lack the precision and generalizability that meta-analysis provides; (b) without pooling across studies, effect estimates may be biased by study-specific factors; and (c) inclusion of single-study factors would create inconsistency in model development methodology.3.
For factors with high unexplained heterogeneity: When meta-analysis revealed I2 > 50% and sensitivity/subgroup analyses could not identify sources of heterogeneity (e.g., total parenteral nutrition (TPN), insertion attempts), these factors were excluded from the prediction model despite being meta-analyzed. This conservative approach prevents incorporating unreliable estimates with high between-study variability.

Final Model Factor Selection Criteria: Of the 41 factors subjected to meta-analysis, only factors meeting ALL the following criteria were included in the prediction model:•
Statistical significance (P < 0.05)•
Based on ≥ 2 studies (meta-analyzed)•
Low-to-moderate heterogeneity (I2 ≤ 50%) OR heterogeneity explainable through subgroup analysis•
Clinically measurable and interpretable
This resulted in 21 independent risk factors included in the final model.

sec.title: Point-score transformation (Sullivan method)
sec.p: We employed the method proposed by Sullivan et al.31 to convert β - coefficients into an integer-based point-scoring system. The scientific basis and process are:

Rationale: Risk prediction models using raw β coefficients require logarithmic calculations at bedside, limiting clinical adoption. The Sullivan method transforms these into simple additive integers while preserving the relative weighting of risk factors. This approach has been successfully implemented in widely-used clinical scores including the Framingham Risk Score.31

Transformation Process:1.
All β coefficients were multiplied by 10 to create scores with one decimal place precision, balancing granularity with simplicity: Point Score = β × 102.
Scores were rounded to one decimal place3.
Total risk score = Sum of points from all present risk factors

Example Calculation:•
Previous VTE event: OR from meta-analysis = 2.18•
β = ln(2.18) = 0.779•
Point score = 0.779 × 10 = 7.79 ≈ 7.8 points
This linear transformation preserves the relative importance (weighting) of each risk factor and the mathematical relationship between predictors and outcome, ensuring the model's validity.

sec.title: Rationale for using subgroup analysis results in model development
sec.p:
When subgroup analyses revealed distinct effect sizes across clinically meaningful subgroups (e.g., hematological vs. solid tumors for D-dimer; breast vs. other surgeries), we incorporated subgroup-specific estimates into the prediction model rather than using overall pooled estimates. This approach is scientifically justified:1.
Biological Plausibility: Different cancer types have fundamentally different coagulation profiles. Hematological malignancies involve bone marrow dysfunction with inherent coagulopathy, while solid tumors primarily cause hypercoagulability through tumor-secreted factors. Using a single pooled OR would obscure this biological heterogeneity and reduce predictive accuracy.2.
Improved Calibration: Subgroup-specific estimates provide more accurate risk predictions. A lymphoma patient with elevated D-dimer faces substantially different risk than a lung cancer patient with elevated D-dimer, which should be reflected in the model.3.
Methodological Precedent: Established prediction models (e.g., Framingham Risk Score) incorporate subgroup-specific estimates when effect modification is present.4.
Statistical Rationale: When interaction testing indicates significant effect modification (P-interaction < 0.10), using subgroup-specific estimates is statistically appropriate and prevents model misspecification.

sec.title: For sensitivity analyses
sec.p: These assessed robustness but did not reveal clinically meaningful effect modification. Therefore, sensitivity analysis results informed decisions about factor inclusion/exclusion (e.g., excluding factors with unstable estimates like insertion attempts, TPN) but did not generate alternative model coefficients.

sec.title: Model validation
sec.p:
A total of 353 patients with cancer who were hospitalized and had PICC catheters placed at the Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences from June 2024 to May 2025 were selected as the source of clinical data. Inclusion criteria included: (1) age ≥ 18 years; (2) diagnosed with cancer; (3) received PICC insertion. Cases with incomplete case information and clinical data were excluded. The PICC-RVT outcome was diagnosed by ultrasonography in the presence of signs and symptoms. On ultrasonic examination, PICC-related thrombosis appeared as an anechoic or hypoechoic image, partially or fully occluding the vessel lumen. Ultimately, after excluding 8 patients were under 18 years old and 7 patients with incomplete clinical data, the final validation population included 338 patients. By reviewing the case records, the risk factors were retrospectively collected. Fig. 1 illustrated the patient selection flowchart for the study.Fig. 1
Process for the selection of patients in the validation population. PICC-RVT, peripherally inserted central catheter -related deep venous thrombosis.Fig. 1

The clinical data were used to examine the generalizability of the risk prediction model, identifying the best implementation strategy. Multivariable logistic regression analysis was performed, the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve was plotted, and the area under curve (AUC) was calculated to estimate the specificity and sensitivity of the predictive model. The optimal cutoff value is determined by maximizing the Youden index. The Hosmer–Lemeshow test assessed the model's goodness of fit. The threshold for statistical significance was set at α = 0.05. All analyses were performed using SPSS software, version 29.0 (IBM Corp.). All performance metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals calculated using 1000 bootstrap resamples to accurately reflect uncertainty given the limited event count. We report multiple calibration metrics including calibration-in-the-large (intercept), calibration slope, and Hosmer–Lemeshow test, recognizing that the latter has limited power with small samples.

The research data collection for the model validation part of this study was approved by the Ethics Committee of National Cancer Center/ Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical Collage (Approval No. 24/164-4444) before it was carried out.

sec.title: Study selection process
sec.p:
A preliminary search of the database identified 2141 potentially relevant articles. Following the removal of duplicates, 1128 unique records were screened based on their titles and abstracts. A comprehensive review was subsequently performed on 58 selected studies. After multiple rounds of rigorous screening, 32 articles were ultimately included in the analysis. The literature screening process and results are illustrated in Fig. 2.Fig. 2
Flow diagram illustrating the original process of screening of studies.Fig. 2

sec.title: Study characteristics and quality assessment
sec.p:
32 articles were included in the final meta-analysis, all of which were published in English between 2012 and 2025. Of these, 22 were retrospective cohort studies, and 10 were prospective cohort studies. Notably, 28 studies (87.5%) were conducted in China, 2 in the UK, and 1 each in the US and Canada, reflecting the geographic concentration of PICC-related thrombosis research. Regarding the study subjects, 5 studies focused on patients with lung cancer, 4 on breast cancer, 3 on lymphatic or hematological malignancies, 1 each on lung cancer and nasopharyngeal cancer, and the cancer types in the remaining studies were not clearly specified. Ultimately, a total of 28813 PICC catheterizations were included in the final analysis, among which 2447 (8.49%) cases of PICC-RVT were identified. The basic characteristics and methodological quality assessment results of the included studies are presented in Table 1.Table 1
Summary characteristics of the included studies (N = 32).Table 1

sec.title: Results of quality assessment
sec.p: According to the GRADE, the evidence level of each risk factor included in the meta-analysis was assessed, and the results indicated that all risk factors were supported by moderate or higher quality evidence. The corresponding results are shown in Supplementary Material (Supplementary Table S3). According to the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS), 28 studies were rated as high quality, while the remaining 4 were considered moderate quality (Supplementary Table S4).

sec.title: Meta-analysis of risk factors for PICC-RVT
sec.p:
The 32 included studies in this systematic review identified a total of 98 statistically significant risk factors associated with PICC-RVT. Among these, 57 risk factors reported solely in single studies were excluded from the meta-analysis due to insufficient data for pooling. The remaining 41 risk factors reported in two or more studies were all subjected to subjected to meta-analysis based on either fixed or random effects model, depending on the heterogeneity of the data. The results of the meta-analysis are presented in Table 2 (The forest plots were shown in Supplementary Fig. S1-S41.Table 2
PICC-RVT risk factors.Table 2
Risk factors for PICC-RVTNo. of studiesSample sizeEffect indexHeterogeneity test
OR (95% CI)P-valueI2, %P-value
Age (> 60 years)541681.72 (1.38–2.16)< 0.0100.40
Sex (male)8131171.37 (1.12–1.69)0.00300.46
BMI (> 25 kg/m2)882572.08 (0.72–6.01)0.1897< 0.01

Hb225970.48 (0.07–3.24)0.4595< 0.01

Catheter malposition or prolapse310424.16 (1.98–8.76)< 0.0100.40
Catheter size229751.94 (0.63–5.93)0.2588< 0.01
Catheter material225022.48 (1.90–3.24)< 0.0100.64
Position of catheter25625.67 (0.85–37.86)0.07810.02
TPN211934.17 (1.12–15.56)0.03530.14
Surgery
Subgroup: Breast surgery222400.32 (0.20–0.54)< 0.0100.91
Subgroup: Others26454.17 (1.50–11.56)< 0.010< 0.01
Radiotherapy289660.93 (0.62–1.39)0.7200.42
Chemotherapy486201.97 (1.52–2.55)< 0.0100.71
Fluorouracil315863.17 (1.62–6.18)< 0.0100.63
Carboplatin229751.56 (0.85–2.86)0.15620.11
Paclitaxel liposome339131.93 (0.76–4.87)0.17630.07
Metastases526722.48 (1.75–3.51)< 0.01260.25
Tumor stage413003.02 (2.18–4.18)< 0.0100.66
Lung cancer360981.23 (0.80–1.87)0.3400.50
Digestive system malignancy360981.30 (0.48–3.47)0.61660.05
Hematological malignancies289663.66 (2.29–5.86)< 0.0100.57
Comorbidities (Diabetes/COPD/Hypertension)1056641.49 (0.94–2.37)0.0969< 0.01

According to the odds ratio values from the meta-analysis, 23 factors showed an independent correlation with the occurrence of PICC-RVT include age, sex, activity amount, self-care disorders, previous VTE event, history of PICC/CVC, infection, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) score, D-Dimer, platelet (PLT), fibrinogen (FIB), triglycerides, myelosuppression, insertion attempts, catheter malposition or prolapse, catheter material, TPN, surgery, chemotherapy, fluorouracil, metastases, tumor stage and hematological malignancies. It should be noted that breast-conserving surgery, as a subgroup analysis within the category of surgical factors, demonstrates a protective effect against PICC-RVT. And the other 18 factors were not significantly associated with PICC-RVT.

For factors exhibiting significant heterogeneity, subgroup analysis were conducted, or a sensitivity analysis were conducted by removing individual articles one by one. The results indicated that the pooled meta-analysis results for factors such as age (> 60 years), sex, previous VTE event and infection remained stable, suggesting robustness of the findings. However, considerable heterogeneity persisted for factors including insertion attempts and TPN.

sec.title: Geographic subgroup analysis
sec.p: Sensitivity analysis comparing Chinese (n = 28) versus non-Chinese studies (n = 4) for commonly reported risk factors revealed no statistically significant interactions (all P-interaction > 0.10), suggesting broadly consistent effect directions across geographic settings. However, confidence intervals for non-Chinese studies were substantially wider due to fewer studies, precluding definitive conclusions about effect magnitude heterogeneity. The limited number of non-Chinese studies represents a key evidence gap warranting future research in diverse Asia–Pacific populations including Japan, South Korea, Australia, and Southeast Asian nations.

sec.title: Model derivation
sec.p: Among the 41 risk factors identified in the systematic review and meta-analysis, 21 risk factors were associated with the incidence of PICC-RVT. We selected the results from subgroup or sensitivity analysis that were most reasonable considering the feasibility of clinical practice. These factors and their combined ORs are as follows: age > 60 (1.72) , male (1.37), less activity amount (4.27), self-care disorders (13.87), previous VTE event (2.18), history of PICC/CVC (3.18), infection (2.84), ECOG score ≥ 3 (3.15), D-Dimer > 0.5 mg/L (6.91 if HM, 3.63 if others), PLT > 300×109/L (3.47), FIB > 4 g/L (1.21), triglycerides ≥ 1.7 mmol/L (1.69), myelosuppression (2.08), catheter malposition or prolapse (4.16), polyurethane (1.71), surgery (0.32 if breast surgery, 4.17 if others), chemotherapy (1.97), fluorouracil (3.17), metastases (2.48), tumor stage of III-IV (3.02), hematological malignancies (3.66). Due to the high heterogeneity across studies involving number of attempts to insert and TPN, these factors were excluded from our risk prediction model.

Overall, a simple PICC-RVT risk prediction model applicable to patients with cancer was developed as follows: age (0 if ≤ 60 and 2.4 if > 60), sex (0 if female and 1.4 if male), activity amount (0 if normal and 6.3 if less), self-care disorders (0 if no and 11.4 if yes), previous VTE event (0 if no and 3.2 if yes), history of PICC/CVC (0 if no and 5.0 if yes), infection (0 if no and 4.5 if yes), ECOG score (0 if 1-2 and 5.0 if ≥ 3), D-Dimer (0 if ≤ 0.5 mg/L, 8.4 if > 0.05 mg/L with HM and 5.6 if > 0.05mg/L with other cancers), PLT (0 if ≤ 300 × 109/L and 5.4 if > 300 × 109 /L), FIB (0 if ≤ 4g/L and 0.8 if > 4g/L), triglycerides (0 if ≤ 1.7 mmol/L and 2.3 if > 1.7mmol/L), myelosuppression (0 if no and 3.2 if yes), catheter malposition or prolapse (0 if no and 6.2 if yes), catheter material (0 if soft silicone and 3.9 if polyurethane), surgery (0 if no, −4.9 if breast surgery and 5.2 if other surgery), chemotherapy (0 if no and 2.9 if yes), use of fluorouracil (0 if no and 5.0 if yes), tumor stage (0 if Ⅰ-Ⅱ and 3.9 if Ⅲ-Ⅳ), lymphoid or hematological malignancies (0 if no and 5.6 if yes) (Table 3).Table 3
PICC-RVT risk prediction model.Table 3
Risk factors for PICC-RVTCategoryPoint
Age (years)> 602.4
SexMale1.4
Activity amountLess6.3
Self-care disordersYes11.4
Previous VTE eventYes3.2
History of PICC/CVCYes5.0
InfectionYes4.5
ECOG score≥ 35.0
D-dimerHM> 0.5 mg/L8.4
Others> 0.5 mg/L5.6
PLT> 300×109/L5.4
FIB> 4 g/L0.8
Triglycerides> 1.7 mmol/L2.3
MyelosuppressionYes3.2
Catheter malposition or prolapseYes6.2
Catheter materialPolyurethane3.9
SurgeryBreast surgeryYes−4.9
OthersYes6.2
ChemotherapyYes2.9
Use of fluorouracilYes5.0
MetastasesYes3.9
Tumor stageIII-IV4.8
Lymphoid or hematological malignanciesYes5.6

sec.title: Model validation
sec.p: The validation population included 338 adult patients with cancer with PICC insertions at our institution between June 2024 and May 2025. Fifteen patients (4.4%) developed PICC-RVT diagnosed by ultrasonography, while 323 (95.6%) did not experience thrombosis. Baseline characteristics are presented in Supplementary Table S2.

The model's area under the ROC curve was 0.792 (95% CI: 0.653–0.931) (Fig. 3), indicating moderate discrimination ability. However, the wide confidence interval reflects substantial uncertainty given the limited event count. The calibration slope was 0.94 (95% CI: 0.42–1.46), suggesting generally appropriate calibration without severe overfitting, though the confidence interval spans from underfitting to mild overfitting, again reflecting estimation imprecision. Calibration-in-the-large was −0.08 (95% CI: −0.52 to 0.36), indicating no systematic overestimation or underestimation. The Hosmer–Lemeshow test yielded P = 0.082, suggesting acceptable calibration, though this test has limited power with 15 events and should be interpreted cautiously.Fig. 3
The area under the ROC curve of the risk prediction model.

At the selected threshold of 23.15, the model achieved sensitivity of 66.7% (10/15 true positives) and specificity of 84.2% (272/323 true negatives), with positive predictive value of 16.4% and negative predictive value of 98.2%.

These preliminary validation results suggest the model may have clinical utility in Chinese patients with cancer receiving PICCs, but several critical limitations must be emphasized. First, with only 15 events, all performance estimates have wide confidence intervals that preclude definitive conclusions. The AUC confidence interval ranges from poor (0.653) to good (0.931) discrimination, and the calibration slope interval spans nearly the full range of plausible values. Second, single-center validation cannot assess geographic transportability. Third, the low event rate (4.4%) compared to reported PICC-RVT incidence (8.6% to 15.3%) suggests potential case ascertainment issues or differential patient selection.

These results should be interpreted as proof-of-concept that the model is potentially applicable in Chinese settings, but definitive conclusions about clinical utility require multi-center validation with ≥ 100 events as recommended by contemporary methodological standards.

sec.title: Development of a clinical prediction tool in electronic medical record systems
sec.p: After the validation of the model, we anticipate that it will serve as a valuable tool in supporting clinical decision-making regarding the appropriateness and safety of PICC use for patients with cancer. We have attempted to develop a PICC-RVT risk calculator for patients with cancer based on the electronic medical record (EMR) system. Specifically, all risk factors included in the model can be automatically retrieved from routinely collected EMR data, thereby enabling immediate risk assessment and providing decision-support for clinical healthcare professionals.

sec.title: Main findings
sec.p: This study developed a PICC-RVT risk prediction model for patients with cancer based on systematic review and meta-analysis of 32 cohort studies, predominantly from Chinese healthcare settings (87.5%). The model incorporates 21 independent risk factors spanning patient characteristics, laboratory markers, catheter-related factors, cancer features, and treatment modalities, guided by Virchow's triad theoretical framework. Preliminary single-center validation in 338 Chinese patients with cancer demonstrated moderate discrimination (AUC 0.792) and acceptable calibration, though with substantial uncertainty due to limited events (n = 15).

The geographic concentration of evidence base and underpowered validation represent key limitations that must be addressed before clinical implementation. However, this model provides the most comprehensive synthesis of PICC-RVT risk factors in patients with cancer to date and offers a framework for risk stratification in Chinese and potentially similar Asia–Pacific healthcare environments. The model's value lies not in claiming universal validity - which contemporary prediction modeling literature increasingly recognizes as unattainable - but in providing region-specific evidence that can inform clinical practice while highlighting critical gaps requiring future research.

sec.title: Implications for nursing practice and research
sec.p: Based on the 32 cohort studies included in the meta-analysis, we ultimately identified 21 independent risk factors for the occurrence of PICC-RVT in patients with cancer. These findings confirm that both complex clinical factors and individual patient condition significantly impact the risk of PICC-RVT. Several of these risk factors can be anticipated in advance and potentially modified, thereby offering a critical opportunity for prevention. Timely and accurate identification of high-risk patients, combined with the formulation of targeted management strategies and the implementation of comprehensive intervention measures, may represent the most effective approach to preventing PICC-RVT and alleviating the associated medical burden.

The results of this study indicate that the intrinsic risk factors for PICC-RVT associated with the patients themselves include advanced age (> 60 years), sex (male), less of activity amount, self-care disorders, performance status, previous VTE event, and history of PICC/CVC. These findings corroborate those reported in several earlier studies. Patients across different age groups and genders may exhibit an elevated risk of PICC-RVT formation due to their physiological differences, lifestyles, and certain underlying health issues.40,54 This phenomenon is primarily attributed to three pathophysiological mechanisms: venous blood stasis, endothelial injury within the venous system, and a hypercoagulable state - all of which contribute to an increased thrombotic risk.10,52 Therefore, it is crucial to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the patient's basic information and condition. Appropriate functional exercises after PICC catheterization can effectively reduce the incidence of complications, such as venous thrombosis and limb swelling. If patients overly restrict limb movement or are unable to move due to self-care deficiencies or reduced physical strength, it may lead to a decrease in blood flow velocity, thereby promoting platelet aggregation on the catheter wall.50,61 Furthermore, patients with cancer with indwelling catheters who have pre-existing medical conditions that impair the integrity of the vascular endothelium or induce a hypercoagulable state, such as a prior history of thrombosis, previous catheterization or infection, are at an increased risk of thrombosis following PICC insertion.47,56

Among the various laboratory indicators, D-Dimer, PLT, FIB and Triglycerides are significantly associated with the incidence of PICC-RVT, which is consistent with previous research findings.62 D-Dimer is a well-recognized biomarker for assessing coagulation and fibrinolytic activity, enabling an indirect evaluation of thrombotic activity and serving as a reliable indicator of the extent of coagulation system activation. The medical community now widely acknowledges that elevated plasma D-Dimer levels are commonly observed in patients with malignancies, a finding that is closely linked to the increased thrombotic risk associated with cancer.50,54,63 Moreover, high platelet count may reflect a tumor-related hypercoagulable state, while elevated fibrinogen indicates the association between inflammation and thrombosis. The results of our meta-analysis further underscore the clinical relevance of these biomarkers in evaluating thrombotic risk.5,38 Studies have also demonstrated that triglyceride levels are positively correlated with the intensity of venous endothelial cell responses to inflammatory cytokines, and that triglycerides can enhance the activation of such cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor, in the vascular intima.64 Therefore, it is necessary to standardize the detection thresholds to enhance their predictive value.

The predominance of Chinese studies (28/32, 87.5%) in our meta-analysis reflects both the concentration of PICC research in Chinese healthcare settings and potential publication bias toward Chinese-language journals indexed in international databases. This geographic concentration raises important questions about model transportability to other Asia–Pacific regions and beyond. Several factors may limit direct application of our model to non-Chinese populations. First, cancer epidemiology differs across Asia–Pacific regions, with gastric and liver cancers more prevalent in East Asia compared to breast and prostate cancers in Australia and New Zealand. Our model's performance in these different case mixes remains unknown. Second, PICC insertion techniques, maintenance protocols, and catheter tip position verification methods vary internationally - Chinese centers may have different protocols for post-insertion care, flushing frequencies, and ultrasound use compared to other countries. Third, baseline thrombotic risk factors differ by population, including hepatitis B prevalence (affecting coagulation pathways), genetic variants in coagulation factors, and medication use patterns. However, the model may have particular relevance for Asia–Pacific regions with healthcare systems and cancer populations similar to China, including Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, and urban centers in Southeast Asia. The biological mechanisms underlying PICC-RVT (Virchow's triad) remain universal, and our subgroup analysis found no statistically significant differences in risk factor effects between Chinese and non-Chinese studies, though power was limited. Future research should prioritize validation in diverse Asia–Pacific populations to assess model transportability. Such validation studies will reveal whether recalibration (adjusting the intercept and slope) or model refitting (re-estimating all coefficients) is necessary for specific populations. Following the principle articulated by Van Calster et al.65 that "there is no such thing as a validated prediction model," we emphasize that our model requires ongoing assessment across geographic settings rather than one-time validation.

In addition, there are some catheter-related risk factors such as catheter material, catheter malposition or prolapse are significantly associated with an increased risk of PICC-RVT. Compared with soft silicone catheters, the utilization of polyurethane catheters is associated with a higher incidence of PICC-RVT.41,54 Polyurethane materials exhibit relatively greater rigidity and stronger surface adhesion compared to soft silicone, and some may contain plasticizers and lack antithrombotic coatings. The elevated thrombotic risk associated with polyurethane catheters results from the synergistic interplay of material characteristics, biological responses, and hemodynamic factors.66 Therefore, a choice of catheter material should be made based on the patient's clinical requirement before catheter insertion. Malposition was another important risk factor of PICC-related UEVT in our study. And another systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated 5636 patients with cancer and found that the risk of UEVT increased in patients with malposition catheter.24,44 Therefore, in clinical practice, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of polyurethane catheters, such as their durability, against the risk of thrombosis, particularly when managing high-risk populations, including patients with cancer. Furthermore, ultrasound imaging can be employed for real-time detection of internal jugular vein malposition during PICC insertion.

The results of this study indicate that among tumor-specific factors, hematological or lymphoid tumors, tumor stage (III-IV), and metastasis significantly increase the risk of PICC-RVT. Due to increased vascular permeability in patients with cancer themselves, various tumor cell secretions (procoagulant substances, tissue factors, mucins, etc.) enter the blood vessels, activating the coagulation mechanism and predisposing the blood to a hypercoagulable state. Additionally, the immunosuppressive condition in patients with cancer often triggers inflammatory responses, with inflammatory mediators being released into the circulation, thereby further promoting thrombus formation.67 Our research findings suggest that hematological or lymphoma patients may have a higher susceptibility to PICC-RVT compared to individuals with other types of malignant tumors. Studies have demonstrated that the incidence of PICC- RVT in lymphoma patients is nearly four times higher than that observed in patients with other malignancies.60 However, further investigation is required to elucidate the underlying mechanisms of this association. According to the TNM staging system, stage III signifies that cancer cells have exhibited extensive local infiltration or significant regional lymph node metastasis, while stage IV indicates the presence of distant metastasis. To date, there are limited studies both domestically and internationally investigating the potential correlation between these stages and the occurrence of thrombosis. However, foreign research has also pointed out that when tumor diseases are in the advanced stage, a hypercoagulable state is more likely to occur, thus increasing the risk of catheter-related thrombosis.10,12 Consistent with previous findings, this study demonstrates that a hypercoagulable state is more likely to develop in patients with advanced-stage or metastatic tumor diseases, thereby increasing the risk of catheter-related thrombosis. However, to date, both national and international research investigating the potential association between disease progression and thrombosis remains limited.

Among the factors related to tumor treatment, both chemotherapy and surgery significantly increase the risk of PICC-related venous thrombosis. Chemotherapy remains an important means of treating malignant tumors, but chemotherapy drugs can damage vascular endothelial cells, cause phlebitis and promote thrombosis, which has been confirmed to significantly increase the risk of PICC-RVT occurrence.40,58 Our study further supports that chemotherapy regimens containing fluorouracil are significantly associated with an increased risk of PICC-RVT.11,37,42 The mechanism underlying fluorouracil-induced thrombosis is complex. Mechanistic studies have shown that 5-fluorouracil can induce vasospasm through the activation of protein kinase C, exert vascular endothelial damage by affecting the production of free radicals, such as nitric oxide and reactive oxygen species, and induce echinocytosis, which may alter blood viscosity.68,69 Myelosuppression is a common complication in patients undergoing chemotherapy, characterized by the fact that greater severity correlates with lower platelet counts and more pronounced coagulation disorders.34,67 Therefore, patients undergoing chemotherapy, especially those receiving fluorouracil chemotherapy, may require more clinical attention, such as more frequent Doppler ultrasound examinations. In addition, surgical operations have also been identified as a risk factor for PICC catheter-related thrombosis.41,50 The mechanisms by which surgery causes thrombosis include damage to vascular endothelial cells, slow blood flow due to anesthesia, and blood in a hypercoagulable state.41,50,70 Interestingly, subgroup analysis revealed that, unlike other tumor surgeries, breast surgery acted as a protective factor against the occurrence of PICC-RVT.11,56 This may be attributed to the fact that, with advances in tumor screening and diagnostic techniques, most breast cancers are detected at an early stage and typically undergo minor and medium-sized surgeries. Furthermore, failure to timely remove the tumor in breast patients with cancer may activate the coagulation cascade and impair the fibrinolytic system, thereby increasing the risk of thrombosis.71

External validation of the prediction model was conducted on 338 adult patients with cancer, demonstrating that the model has strong predictive power, with an AUC of 0.792 (95% CI: 0.653–0.931). We selected a cumulative score of 23.15 as the cut-off value, indicating relatively high risk, with the sensitivity and specificity of 0.667 and 0.842. The Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test indicated that the model had a satisfactory prediction effect, and no further calibration is required. By calculating risk score, it was easy to distinguish high-risk patients from other populations. Overall, the risk predictive models for PICC-RVT in adult patients with cancer incorporate findings from previous studies and demonstrate sensitivity, specificity, and clinical usefulness in the validation population.

This study innovatively employed the meta-analysis method to systematically summarize evidence from several aspects such as the demographic characteristics, laboratory data, treatment regimens, progression of disease and catheter-related factors in patients with cancer, thereby identifying potential associations with the risk of PICC-RVT. Compared with the widely used machine learning algorithms at present, this method has the advantages of strong interpretability of results and no "black box" problem. Of course, we do not rule out the introduction of new predictive tools in subsequent studies for comparison and verification, in order to seek more accurate and reliable risk prediction schemes. Finally, we also attempted to develop a risk calculation tool for PICC-RVT in patients with cancer based on the EMR system, enabling rapid and automated risk assessment and providing real-time and objective decision support for clinical medical staff.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: First, although all included studies were published in English, the majority (28 out of 32) originated from China, which may introduce regional bias. Secondly, some important factors were excluded due to high heterogeneity, which may lead to the omission of key predictive variables. Thirdly, most of the studies were retrospective designs, which carry the risk of selection bias and insufficient control of confounding factors. In future research, emphasis should also be placed on: ① Further multicenter prospective cohort studies should be conducted to verify the predictive effect of the model. In addition, machine learning algorithms can be introduced, and large-sample validation and model calibration can be carried out. While our search strategy employed broad condition terms and multiple complementary approaches to maximize retrieval, we cannot exclude the possibility that some relevant studies without explicit risk terminology in titles/abstracts may have been missed.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Based on systematic review and meta-analysis, this study identified the key risk factors for PICC-RVT and developed a predictive model including 21 specific factors. Further research on these factors is necessary to provide comprehensive and reliable evidence for the management of PICC-RVT. By integrating multi-dimensional risk factors, this study provides a theoretical basis for the precise prevention and control of PICC-RVT in patients with cancer. The model was validated with an external cohort. While the model's predictive efficiency needs further improvement, it has significant clinical applicability and promotion potential.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Zhang Jiwen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – Original Draft. Li Caiyun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – Original Draft. Zhang Huijuan: Data Curation, Methodology, Supervision. Wu Wenbo: Data Curation, Methodology. Sun Xiaoshuang: Validation. Zhang Wei: Resources, Conceptualization, Supervision, Project Administration, Funding Acquisition and Writing – Review & Editing. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Ethics statement
sec.p: This study was conducted after obtaining approval from the Research Ethics Review Committee of National Cancer Center/ Cancer Hospital, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical Collage (Approval No. 24/164-4444).

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and/or its supplementary materials.

sec.title: Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
sec.p: No AI tools/services were used during the preparation of this work.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This study was supported by the CAMS Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences (CIFMS) (Grant No. 2023-I2M-C&T-B-099). The funders had no role in considering the study design or in the collection, analysis, interpretation of data, writing of the report, or decision to submit the article for publication.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflict of interest.
